title: Put it into the world
blocks.0.text: You built a site. It is on your computer, in one file, and nobody but you can see it.
blocks.1.text: This is the last step and it takes five minutes.
blocks.3.text: There is an official tool that reads HTML and tells you what is broken. It is free, it has been around for twenty years, and people who do this professionally open it every day.
blocks.5.text: You open it, paste your code, press Check. It gives you a list: an unclosed tag, a missing alt, two identical ids, a tag nested in the wrong place.
blocks.6.text: It will not tell you that you used a <div> instead of an <article> — that is meaning, not an error. But it will catch every broken bracket your eye skipped.
blocks.7.text: Nobody writes HTML without mistakes. People simply find them before anyone else does.
blocks.9.text: Your site needs an address. A real one, that you can send to someone who will open it on their phone on the bus.
blocks.10.text: There are three places where this happens free and in minutes. All of them give you an address, work with a real domain later, and ask for no card.
blocks.11.items.0: Netlify — the easiest. You drag the folder with your files onto their site. Done. Literally that.
blocks.11.items.1: GitHub Pages — you need a GitHub account, but you also learn something you will need for the rest of your life.
blocks.11.items.2: Vercel — the same as Netlify, a little more for developers.
blocks.12.text: Your file must be called index.html. That is the name every server looks for when someone opens the bare address. Call it mysite.html and you will have to type that into the address.
blocks.14.text: You will get an address. Something like your-site.netlify.app. Open it on your phone.
blocks.15.text: Now you will see everything I told you about and the preview could not show. The icon in the tab. The title. How the page sits on a phone, because you added the viewport. What the link looks like when you send it to someone.
blocks.16.text: All the invisible things suddenly become visible. That is why they were there.
blocks.18.text: Not to be praised. For something else.
blocks.19.text: Until now everything you made lived in a box. A lesson, a preview, a check. Now it is out there and anyone can open it. You are no longer someone learning HTML. You are someone with a site.
blocks.20.text: The difference between the two is not knowledge. It is that you pressed Upload.
blocks.22.text: Your site is ugly. You know why and you know what fixes it.
blocks.23.text: But notice what you will NOT be doing: rewriting anything. CSS goes on top of this very code. Your skeleton is done and it is a good one — it will carry whatever you put on it.
blocks.24.text: That is exactly why we went through all of it. Not so you would know the tags. So you would have something to build on.
